title: Minor Burn
check_first: Is the skin red but not blistered?
Is the burn smaller than the person's palm?
Is it away from the face, hands, feet, or genitals?
do_now: Cool the burn under cool (not cold) running water for 10–20 minutes
Remove jewelry or tight clothing near the burn
Cover loosely with a sterile non-fluffy dressing or cling wrap
Give paracetamol or ibuprofen for pain relief if appropriate
do_not: Do not use ice, butter, toothpaste, or oily substances
Do not burst any blisters that form
Do not remove clothing stuck to the burn
Do not cover with fluffy cotton wool
when_to_call: Burn is larger than the person's palm
Burn is on the face, hands, feet, genitals, or over a major joint
Signs of infection appear (increasing pain, redness, swelling, fever)
The person is a child under 5 or elderly

title: Severe Burn
check_first: Is the burn deep, charred, or affecting multiple layers of skin?
Is the burn on the face, hands, feet, genitals, or airway (smoke inhalation)?
Is the person showing signs of shock (pale, cold, rapid breathing)?
do_now: Call emergency services immediately (911/999/112)
Cool the burn under cool running water for up to 20 minutes if safe to do so
Lay the person down and elevate legs if not a face or neck burn
Keep the person warm with a blanket — avoid covering the burn
Monitor breathing and be ready to perform CPR
do_not: Do not remove clothing stuck to burned skin
Do not apply any creams, gels, or ice
Do not leave the person alone
Do not give anything by mouth if they may need surgery
when_to_call: Always call emergency services for severe burns
Difficulty breathing or suspected smoke inhalation
Person becomes unconscious or unresponsive
Signs of shock present

title: Minor Bleeding (Cut/Wound)
check_first: Is the bleeding slow and controllable?
Is the wound shallow without embedded objects?
Is there any sign of infection (redness, warmth, swelling, pus)?
do_now: Wash your hands or put on gloves if available
Apply firm direct pressure with a clean cloth or sterile dressing
Maintain pressure for at least 10 minutes without lifting
Once bleeding stops, clean the wound gently with clean water
Apply a sterile dressing or plaster
do_not: Do not remove an embedded object — stabilize it in place
Do not lift the dressing to check — apply more on top
Do not use a tourniquet for minor bleeding
when_to_call: Bleeding does not stop after 10 minutes of firm pressure
Wound is deep, gaping, or contains foreign objects
Signs of infection develop within days
Wound is from an animal bite or puncture

title: Severe Bleeding
check_first: Is the bleeding heavy, spurting, or not controllable?
Is the person pale, cold, sweating, or confused (signs of shock)?
Is there an object embedded in the wound?
do_now: Call emergency services immediately (911/999/112)
Apply very firm direct pressure with the cleanest material available
If a limb, apply a tourniquet 5–7 cm above the wound if bleeding cannot be controlled
Note the time the tourniquet was applied
Lay the person down, elevate legs if no spinal injury suspected
Keep them warm and reassure them while waiting for help
do_not: Do not remove an embedded object
Do not release tourniquet once applied — wait for paramedics
Do not give anything to eat or drink
when_to_call: Always call emergency services for severe uncontrolled bleeding
Person becomes unconscious
Signs of shock: pale, rapid breathing, confusion, cold skin

title: Choking — Adult
check_first: Can the person speak, cough, or breathe at all?
Are they clutching their throat? Turning blue?
Is it a mild obstruction (coughing) or severe (no air)?
do_now: Encourage forceful coughing if the person can cough
If coughing fails: give up to 5 firm back blows between shoulder blades with heel of hand
If back blows fail: give up to 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver) — stand behind, push inward and upward
Alternate 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts
If person becomes unconscious: call 911/999/112 and begin CPR
do_not: Do not perform blind finger sweeps in the mouth
Do not use abdominal thrusts on pregnant women or infants — use chest thrusts instead
Do not slap the back if the person is still coughing effectively
when_to_call: Object cannot be dislodged after several cycles of back blows and thrusts
Person becomes unconscious or stops breathing
Person is pregnant or obese (modified technique needed)

title: Choking — Infant (Under 1 Year)
check_first: Is the infant making any sound, breathing, or coughing?
Is the infant's face turning red, then blue?
Is the infant limp or unresponsive?
do_now: Hold infant face-down along your forearm supporting the head
Give 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand
Turn infant face-up, supporting the head, and give 5 chest thrusts with 2 fingers on the breastbone
Check mouth and remove any visible object only if you can see it
Alternate 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts
If unconscious: call 999/911/112 and start infant CPR
do_not: Do not perform abdominal thrusts (Heimlich) on infants
Do not do a blind finger sweep — only remove an object if clearly visible
Do not shake the infant
when_to_call: Immediately call 911/999/112 if object cannot be dislodged
Infant becomes unresponsive
Infant stops breathing

title: Fracture (Broken Bone)
check_first: Is the area painful, swollen, bruised, or deformed?
Can the person move the injured limb?
Is a bone visible through the skin (open fracture)?
do_now: Keep the person still and do not move the injured limb
Immobilize the limb in the position found using padding or a splint
Apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling
Elevate the limb if possible and if it doesn't cause more pain
Seek medical help — most fractures need X-ray and professional treatment
do_not: Do not try to straighten or realign the bone
Do not apply direct pressure to the bone
Do not move the person if a spinal injury is suspected
when_to_call: Open fracture (bone visible through skin)
Suspected spinal or pelvis fracture
Person is in severe pain, unconscious, or in shock
Fracture involves femur (thigh bone) — major blood loss risk

title: Seizure
check_first: Is the person convulsing or having uncontrolled muscle movements?
How long has the seizure been happening?
Do you know if the person has epilepsy?
do_now: Keep calm — most seizures last less than 3 minutes
Protect the person from injury: clear hard objects away, cushion the head
Time the seizure from when it starts
Turn the person onto their side (recovery position) when convulsions stop
Stay with them and reassure them as they regain consciousness
do_not: Do not restrain the person or hold them down
Do not put anything in their mouth
Do not give water or food until fully conscious
when_to_call: Seizure lasts more than 5 minutes
A second seizure follows without the person recovering
Person does not regain consciousness
Person is injured, pregnant, or diabetic
First-ever seizure — always seek medical assessment

title: Asthma Attack
check_first: Is the person wheezing, coughing, or having difficulty breathing?
Do they have their reliever inhaler (usually blue)?
Are they getting worse despite using the inhaler?
do_now: Help the person sit upright in a comfortable position — do not lay them down
Help them use their reliever (blue) inhaler — one puff every 30–60 seconds, up to 10 puffs
If using a spacer device, attach it to the inhaler
Stay calm and reassure them — panic worsens breathing
If no improvement after 10 puffs: call 999/911/112
do_not: Do not lay the person down
Do not leave them alone
Do not use a preventer (brown/purple) inhaler during an attack
when_to_call: No improvement after 10 puffs of reliever inhaler
Person is too breathless to speak in full sentences
Lips or fingertips turn blue (cyanosis)
Person becomes exhausted, confused, or loses consciousness

title: Cardiac Arrest (CPR)
check_first: Is the person unresponsive — no reaction to shout or touch?
Are they not breathing normally (no chest rise, gasping, or no breath)?
Call for an AED while starting CPR
do_now: Call 999/911/112 immediately and ask for an AED
Start chest compressions: place one hand on top of the other on the center of the chest
Push hard and fast — at least 5–6 cm deep, at a rate of 100–120 per minute
Give 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions (if trained)
If untrained or unwilling: perform hands-only CPR (continuous compressions)
Use the AED as soon as it arrives — follow the voice instructions
Continue until help arrives or the person shows signs of life
do_not: Do not stop compressions unless the AED is ready to analyze
Do not move the person unless there is immediate danger
Do not wait — every second without CPR reduces survival
when_to_call: Always call emergency services immediately
Whenever a person is unresponsive and not breathing normally

title: Stroke — Use FAST
check_first: FACE: Is one side of the face drooping or numb?
ARMS: Can they raise both arms equally?
SPEECH: Is their speech slurred or confused?
TIME: Time is critical — act now
do_now: Call 999/911/112 immediately — stroke is a time-critical emergency
Note the exact time symptoms started
Keep the person calm, still, and comfortable — do not give food or water
Loosen any tight clothing around the neck and chest
If unconscious but breathing: place in recovery position and monitor
Be ready to perform CPR if breathing stops
do_not: Do not give aspirin unless specifically instructed by emergency services
Do not give anything to eat or drink — swallowing may be impaired
Do not leave the person alone
when_to_call: Call immediately if ANY of the FAST signs are present
Even if symptoms seem to pass — a TIA (mini-stroke) needs urgent assessment

title: Severe Allergic Reaction (Anaphylaxis)
check_first: Is the person experiencing swelling of the throat, lips, or tongue?
Do they have hives, redness, and difficulty breathing?
Have they been exposed to a known allergen (e.g., bee sting, peanuts, medication)?
do_now: Call 999/911/112 immediately
If the person has an EpiPen (adrenaline auto-injector): use it now on the outer thigh
Help them sit in a comfortable position — upright if breathing difficulty, lying down if faint
A second EpiPen can be given 5–15 minutes later if no improvement
Monitor breathing and be ready to perform CPR
Keep them warm and calm while waiting for help
do_not: Do not make the person stand or walk
Do not give antihistamines as a substitute for an EpiPen — they act too slowly
Do not leave the person alone
when_to_call: Always call 999/911/112 — anaphylaxis is life-threatening
Even if EpiPen was used — effects are temporary
Person may deteriorate again after initial improvement (biphasic reaction)
